title: Authors and Citation
text: Dyce J, Singh (2026). caretMultimodal: Multimodal Late Fusion R. R package version 1.0, https://github.com/CompBio-Lab/caretMultimodal.
code: @Manual{,   title = {caretMultimodal: Multimodal Late Fusion in R},   author = {Josh Dyce and Amrit Singh},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0},   url = {https://github.com/CompBio-Lab/caretMultimodal}, }

title: Multimodal Late Fusion in R
text: caretMultimodal extends caret framework support late fusion workflows R, enabling users train models independently across multiple data modalities combine predictions single meta-model. Designed R developers, data scientists, biomedical researchers, caretMultimodal makes late fusion ensemble modelling accessible flexible single-dataset workflows caret. Example late fusion workflow using cross-validation

title: Multimodal Late Fusion in R
text: Includes functionality caret, giving users full control sampling strategies, training methods, hyperparameter tuning, Default cross-validation structure careful handling prevent data leakage across modalities Late fusion ensembling using stacked generalization Parallelization faster training across models datasets Model trimming reduce memory usage large ensembles Built-evaluation tools performance assessment, ROC curves, variable importance Detailed error messages simplify debugging

title: Multimodal Late Fusion in R
text: Full API documentation available compbio-lab.github.io/caretMultimodal

title: Multimodal Late Fusion in R
text: package can installed using devtools
code: devtools::install_github("CompBio-Lab/caretMultimodal")

title: Multimodal Late Fusion in R
text: project structure inspired Zach Mayer’s caretEnsemble package, used stacking multiple models single dataset.

title: Construct a caret_list object — caret_list
text: Builds list caret::train objects, model corresponds data set data_list. resulting list used input caret_stack() construct meta model.

title: Construct a caret_list object — caret_list
code: caret_list(   target,   data_list,   method,   identifier_column_name = NULL,   trControl = NULL,   metric = NULL,   trim = TRUE,   do_parallel = TRUE,   ... )

title: Construct a caret_list object — caret_list
text: target Target vector, either numeric regression factor/character classification. data_list named list matrix-like objects, element dataset train separate model . Names preserved returned caret_list unique. method method train models . Can custom method one found caret::modelLookup(). identifier_column_name string giving name column links rows across datasets (e.g. participant ID). provided, column must present datasets data_list proper row matching. Use datasets different numbers rows. NULL, datasets assumed identical rows order. Default NULL. Note: Providing identifier_column_name disables support custom trControl. new resampling scheme must constructed dataset row matching, may invalidate user-supplied fold indices. trControl Control use caret::train function. NULL, .default_control() used construct default control (5-fold cross validation) depending target type. Default NULL. metric Metric use caret::train function. NULL, .default_metric() used construct default metric depending target type. Default NULL. trim Logical, whether trained models trimmed save memory. Default TRUE. do_parallel Logical, whether parallelize model training across datasets. Default TRUE. ... Additional arguments pass caret::train function (e.g. tuneGrid = tuneGrid).

title: Construct a caret_list object — caret_list
text: caret_list object (named list trained caret::train models corresponding data_list).

title: Construct a caret_list object — caret_list
code: set.seed(42)  data(heart_failure_datasets)  data_list <-  heart_failure_datasets[c("cells", "holter", "mrna", "proteins")]  # Define hyperparameters to tune (optional) tuneGrid <- expand.grid(alpha = 0.5, lambda = c(0.01, 0.1))  # Construct caret_list object base_models <- caret_list(   target = heart_failure_datasets$demo$hospitalizations,   data_list = data_list,   method = "glmnet",   tuneGrid = tuneGrid ) #> Loading required package: ggplot2 #> Loading required package: lattice  class(base_models) #> [1] "caret_list" "list"

title: Construct a caret_stack object. — caret_stack
text: Train ensemble (stacked) model base learners caret_list. ensemble caret::train model learns combine predictions base models. default, meta-learner trained --fold predictions resampling process, ensuring ensemble overfit -sample predictions. Alternatively, new datasets can supplied via data_list target transfer-learning style ensembling.

title: Construct a caret_stack object. — caret_stack
code: caret_stack(   caret_list,   method,   data_list = NULL,   target = NULL,   trControl = NULL,   metric = NULL,   ... )

title: Construct a caret_stack object. — caret_stack
text: caret_list caret_list object method method train ensemble model. Can custom method one found caret::modelLookup(). data_list list datasets predict , dataset matching corresponding model caret_list. NULL, --fold predictions base models used. Default NULL. target Target parameter vector must provided predicting new data list. NULL, target vector used train base models used. trControl Control use caret::train function. NULL, .default_control() used construct default control (5-fold cross validation) depending target type. Default NULL. metric Metric use caret::train function. NULL, .default_metric() used construct default metric depending target type. Default NULL. ... Additional arguments pass caret::train

title: Construct a caret_stack object. — caret_stack
text: caret_stack object.

title: Construct a caret_stack object. — caret_stack
code: set.seed(42)  data(heart_failure_datasets)  data_list <-  heart_failure_datasets[c("cells", "holter", "mrna", "proteins")]  # Define hyperparameters to tune (optional) tuneGrid <- expand.grid(alpha = 0.5, lambda = c(0.01, 0.1))  # Construct caret_list object base_models <- caret_list(   target = heart_failure_datasets$demo$hospitalizations,   data_list = data_list,   method = "glmnet",   tuneGrid = tuneGrid )  # Train a Random Forest stacked model on the out-of-fold predictions from the base models stacked_model <- caret_stack(   caret_list = base_models,   method = "rf" )  class(stacked_model) #> [1] "caret_stack"

what: Perform an ablation analysis for a caret_stack model. — compute_ablation.caret_stack
title: Perform an ablation analysis for a caret_stack model. — compute_ablation.caret_stack
text: function performs systematic ablation analysis caret_stack ensemble evaluate base learner's contribution predictive performance. procedure begins full set base model predictions iteratively removes one model time. iteration, ensemble model retrained remaining learners using method, tuneGrid, trControl settings original stack. Model importance ensemble estimated, learner lowest (highest, reverse = TRUE) relative contribution removed. ablation step, --fold predictions generated evaluated using user-supplied metric_function, accept two arguments (predictions, target) return single numeric value (e.g., RMSE, accuracy, AUC). resulting performance metrics model contribution estimates recorded stage returned data.table.

title: Perform an ablation analysis for a caret_stack model. — compute_ablation.caret_stack
code: # S3 method for class 'caret_stack' compute_ablation(object, metric_function, metric_name, reverse = FALSE, ...)

title: Perform an ablation analysis for a caret_stack model. — compute_ablation.caret_stack
text: object caret_stack object. metric_function function takes two arguments (predictions, target) returns single numeric value representing metric compute (e.g., RMSE, accuracy, AUC). metric_name name metric reverse direction ablate . FALSE, lowest contributing model removed iteration. TRUE, highest contributing model removed. Default FALSE. ... used. Included S3 compatibility.

title: Perform an ablation analysis for a caret_stack model. — compute_ablation.caret_stack
text: data.table

title: Perform an ablation analysis for a caret_stack model. — compute_ablation.caret_stack
code: # Load pre-trained example caret_stack object data(heart_failure_stack)  # Since the example stack is a binary classifier, # this metric function needs to take in predictions (floats) and # ground truth (binary vector), and produce a single number. metric_fun <- function(preds, target) {   pROC::roc(response = target, predictor = preds, quiet = TRUE)$auc }  compute_ablation(heart_failure_stack, metric_fun, "AUC") #> [1] "cells" #>         Row Ablation_1 #>      <char>      <num> #> 1:    cells  0.0000000 #> 2:   holter 44.1022985 #> 3:     mrna 35.3940024 #> 4: proteins 20.5036991 #> 5:      AUC  0.9145299 #> [1] "proteins" #>         Row Ablation_1 Ablation_2 #>      <char>      <num>      <num> #> 1:    cells  0.0000000         NA #> 2:   holter 44.1022985 44.0964872 #> 3:     mrna 35.3940024 35.0032406 #> 4: proteins 20.5036991 20.9002722 #> 5:      AUC  0.9145299  0.9316239 #> [1] "mrna" #>         Row Ablation_1 Ablation_2 Ablation_3 #>      <char>      <num>      <num>      <num> #> 1:    cells  0.0000000         NA         NA #> 2:   holter 44.1022985 44.0964872 58.8999619 #> 3:     mrna 35.3940024 35.0032406 41.1000381 #> 4: proteins 20.5036991 20.9002722         NA #> 5:      AUC  0.9145299  0.9316239  0.8393162 #>         Row Ablation_1 Ablation_2 Ablation_3 #>      <char>      <num>      <num>      <num> #> 1:    cells  0.0000000         NA         NA #> 2:   holter 44.1022985 44.0964872 58.8999619 #> 3:     mrna 35.3940024 35.0032406 41.1000381 #> 4: proteins 20.5036991 20.9002722         NA #> 5:      AUC  0.9145299  0.9316239  0.8393162

what: Compute the feature level contributions for a caret_stack model. — compute_feature_contributions.caret_stack
title: Compute the feature level contributions for a caret_stack model. — compute_feature_contributions.caret_stack
text: Feature-level contributions ensemble model computed using two-stage application caret::varImp. First, varImp applied ensemble model, base-model predictions treated features, yielding dataset-level weights. Next, varImp applied base model obtain feature-level importance scores within dataset. final contribution individual feature ensemble calculated product dataset-level weight feature-level importance within corresponding base model.

title: Compute the feature level contributions for a caret_stack model. — compute_feature_contributions.caret_stack
code: # S3 method for class 'caret_stack' compute_feature_contributions(object, n_features = 20, ...)

title: Compute the feature level contributions for a caret_stack model. — compute_feature_contributions.caret_stack
text: object caret_stack object. n_features maximum number features include. Setting large value include features. Default 20. ... used. Included S3 compatibility.

title: Compute the feature level contributions for a caret_stack model. — compute_feature_contributions.caret_stack
text: data.table

code: # Load pre-trained example caret_stack object data(heart_failure_stack)  compute_feature_contributions(heart_failure_stack) #>        Model              Feature Relative Contribution #>       <char>               <char>                 <num> #>  1:   holter HS_HR_AVE_DayToNight             41.585315 #>  2:     mrna                 TLR7              7.210145 #>  3:     mrna                 PRLR              5.048845 #>  4:     mrna               FLVCR2              4.168204 #>  5: proteins                ITIH2              4.114475 #>  6:   holter    HR_SECONDS_MAX_RR              2.516984 #>  7:     mrna               SLC8A1              2.398912 #>  8:     mrna              RETREG1              2.088714 #>  9:     mrna                 TTC9              2.064062 #> 10: proteins                  PLG              1.965363 #> 11:     mrna                FOLR2              1.749651 #> 12:     mrna                 SVBP              1.738274 #> 13:     mrna                 NT5E              1.614556 #> 14:     mrna                 ERFE              1.390788 #> 15: proteins                 PON3              1.248055 #> 16: proteins                  B2M              1.200775 #> 17: proteins             SERPINA4              1.065383 #> 18: proteins                 CST3              1.047414 #> 19: proteins                 ANGT              1.039845 #> 20:     mrna             ANKRD36B              1.029405 #>        Model              Feature Relative Contribution #>       <char>               <char>                 <num>

title: Compute metrics with a provided metric function — compute_metric.caret_stack
text: object caret_stack object metric_function function takes two arguments (predictions, target) returns single numeric value representing metric compute (e.g., RMSE, accuracy, AUC). metric_name name metric descending Whether sort descending order. FALSE, output sorted ascending order. Default TRUE. ... used. Included S3 compatibility.

title: Compute metrics with a provided metric function — compute_metric.caret_stack
text: data.table metrics

title: Compute metrics with a provided metric function — compute_metric.caret_stack
code: # Load pre-trained example caret_stack object data(heart_failure_stack)  # Since the example stack is a binary classifier, # this metric function needs to take in predictions (floats) and # ground truth (binary vector), and produce a single number. metric_fun <- function(preds, target) {   pROC::roc(response = target, predictor = preds, quiet = TRUE)$auc }  compute_metric(heart_failure_stack, metric_fun, "AUC") #>       Model       AUC #>      <char>     <num> #> 1:    cells 0.7435897 #> 2:   holter 0.7641026 #> 3:     mrna 0.7829060 #> 4: proteins 0.8786325 #> 5: ensemble 0.9145299

title: Heart Failure Datasets — heart_failure_datasets
text: multimodal dataset Singh et al. (2019) containing demographic, cellular, electrophysiological, molecular features predicting cardiac-related hospitalizations. Used examples throughout caretMultimodal package.

title: Heart Failure Datasets — heart_failure_datasets
code: heart_failure_datasets

title: Heart Failure Datasets — heart_failure_datasets
text: named list 5 elements: demo data.frame demographic features cells data.frame cell count features holter data.frame Holter monitor (ECG) features mrna data.frame mRNA expression features proteins data.frame protein abundance features

title: Heart Failure Datasets — heart_failure_datasets
text: Singh et al. Ensembling Electrical Proteogenomics Biomarkers Improved Prediction Cardiac-Related 3-Month Hospitalizations: Pilot Study. Can J Cardiol. 2019 Apr. doi:10.1016/j.cjca.2018.11.021

what: Out-of-fold predictions from a caret_list — oof_predictions.caret_list
title: Out-of-fold predictions from a caret_list — oof_predictions.caret_list
text: Retrieve --fold predictions corresponding best hyperparameter setting trained caret models. predictions come resampling process (final refit) can optionally aggregated across resamples produce single prediction per training instance.

title: Out-of-fold predictions from a caret_list — oof_predictions.caret_list
code: # S3 method for class 'caret_list' oof_predictions(   object,   drop_redundant_class = TRUE,   aggregate_resamples = TRUE,   intersection_only = TRUE,   ... )

title: Out-of-fold predictions from a caret_list — oof_predictions.caret_list
text: object caret_list object drop_redundant_class Logical, whether exclude first class level prediction output. Default TRUE. aggregate_resamples Logical, whether aggregate resamples across folds. Default TRUE. intersection_only Logical, whether trim --fold predictions intersection samples present across models list (.e., intersection training indices used resampling). Default TRUE. ... used. Included S3 compatibility.

what: Out-of-fold predictions from a caret_stack — oof_predictions.caret_stack
title: Out-of-fold predictions from a caret_stack — oof_predictions.caret_stack
text: Retrieve --fold predictions corresponding best hyperparameter setting trained ensemble model. predictions come resampling process (final refit) can optionally aggregated across resamples produce single prediction per training instance. base model predictions returned training data ensemble; depending model setup, may true --fold predictions simply fitted values. classification models, predictions always exclude first class index.
